title: What to Wear to an On-Campus Interview or Career Fair
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You have a career fair tomorrow. You open your closet. Nothing looks right. Everything feels wrong. You don't want to be overdressed. You don't want to be underdressed. You have no idea what 'business casual' means. Here is the simple formula for dressing professionally as a student without spending a lot of money.

The Simple Formula

Business casual = a step above everyday clothes, a step below a suit. For students, that means:

• Button-down shirt or blouse
• Slacks, chinos, or a skirt
• Closed-toe shoes (no sneakers)
• Optional: blazer or cardigan

This works for almost every campus career fair and on-campus interview. Don't overcomplicate it.

For Men: The Starter Outfit

Shirt: Solid color button-down. Light blue, white, or pale pink. Avoid bright colors and bold patterns. Long sleeves are better than short. Tuck it in.

Pants: Chinos or dress slacks. Khaki, navy, gray, or black. No jeans. No cargo pockets. No sweatpants.

Shoes: Leather or faux leather. Oxfords, loafers, or clean desert boots. No sneakers. No sandals. No boots with lugs.

Belt: Match your shoe color. Black belt with black shoes. Brown belt with brown shoes. This small detail matters more than you think.

Optional blazer: A navy blazer upgrades any outfit. You can find affordable ones at H&M, Uniqlo, or thrift stores. Not required but helpful.

For Women: The Starter Outfit

Top: Blouse, button-down, or a nice sweater. Avoid low necklines. Avoid sheer fabrics. Avoid t-shirts with logos.

Bottoms: Slacks, chinos, or a knee-length skirt. Pencil skirts are professional. Avoid short skirts, tight skirts, or casual fabrics like jersey.

Shoes: Flats, loafers, low heels, or oxfords. Avoid sneakers, sandals, and very high heels. You'll be standing and walking. Comfort matters.

Blazer or cardigan: A structured blazer looks polished. A cardigan is more casual but still professional. Either works.

What to Avoid at All Costs

• Jeans (even dark ones — just don't)
• T-shirts (with logos, graphics, or text)
• Hoodies or sweatshirts
• Sneakers (even clean white ones — save them for another day)
• Shorts (any length)
• Flip-flops or sandals
• Hats (unless religious or medical)
• Visible undergarments (straps, bra edges, boxers)
• Strong perfume or cologne (some people are sensitive)

The Tech/Startup Exception

If you're recruiting for a tech startup or a very casual company, the rules relax. You might be fine in dark jeans, a nice sweater, and clean sneakers. But when in doubt, dress up. Being slightly overdressed is rarely a problem. Being underdressed is memorable for the wrong reason.

How to Do It on a Budget

Thrift stores: You can find blazers, dress shirts, and slacks for $5-10 each. Get them tailored ($15-20) and they'll fit like they're expensive.

Borrow: Ask a friend or family member. Someone has clothes they never wear.

Buy basics: One pair of navy slacks, one white button-down, one blazer. That's three items. You can mix and match for years.

You don't need a $500 suit. You need to look clean, put-together, and respectful. Recruiters know you're a student. They're not expecting perfection. They are expecting effort. Show it.
